content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob are playing a game on a 2×22 \times 22×2 grid. Each cell (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) initially contains a non-negative number of stones AijA_{ij}Aij​.


Alice moves first.
On each move, the current player must remove exactly one stone from one cell that lies in the same row or the same column as the cell used in the immediately previous move.
On the first move, there is no restriction — Alice may remove one stone from any cell. A cell may be chosen multiple times as long as it still has at least one stone.
The game ends immediately when the player to move has no legal move, i.e., when every cell in the row and column of the last chosen cell is empty (has zero stones).
Alice wants to maximize the total number of stones left on the board when the game ends, and Bob wants to minimize the total number of stones left on the board when the game ends.

Given the initial counts in the 2×22 \times 22×2 grid, determine the number of stones remaining on the board after the game ends under optimal play.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line containing four space-separated integers A11,A12,A21,A22A_{11}, A_{12}, A_{21}, A_{22}A11​,A12​,A21​,A22​ — where AijA_{ij}Aij​ represents the number of stones in the cell (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) of the 2×22 \times 22×2 grid.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer — the number of stones remaining on the board after the game ends under optimal play.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The grid is [1111]\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}[11​11​].
Here is one example of how the game can go:

Suppose Alice chooses (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) on her first move; making the grid [0111]\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}[01​11​].
Bob then chooses (1,2)(1, 2)(1,2) on his move, which lies in the same row; making the grid [0011]\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}[01​01​].
Alice is then forced to choose (2,2)(2, 2)(2,2), since that's the only cell containing a positive number of stones in the same row/column. The grid is now [0010]\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}[01​00​].
Finally, Bob chooses (2,1)(2, 1)(2,1). The grid is [0000]\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}[00​00​], and the game ends since no more moves are possible.

Though Alice wants to maximize the count of remaining stones, it can be shown that no matter what she does, Bob can force all stones to be removed; so the answer here is 000.
